_purpose: Hidden History profile for the WI Southern Driftless cluster. Documents the stories that official records obscure, minimize, or omit — Ho-Chunk removal and resilient return, Black Hawk War violence, lead mining health costs, anti-German coercion during WWI, Depression-era milk strikes, and the systemic erasures that shaped what survives in the archive.

hidden_events.description: The Ho-Chunk (Winnebago) people were the original inhabitants of the Southern Driftless. After the Black Hawk War (1832), the federal government forced a series of removals — to Iowa (1840), to Minnesota (1848), to South Dakota (1863), and to Nebraska. Each removal was supposed to be final. Each time, Ho-Chunk families walked back to Wisconsin, refusing to leave permanently. By the 1870s, the government effectively gave up on removal, and scattered Ho-Chunk families lived on marginal land throughout the Driftless — in the coulees, on sandy floodplains, on the land that white settlers did not want. They appear in county records sporadically and inconsistently. The Ho-Chunk Nation persisted in the Driftless through a century of removal attempts, and their presence is the most significant hidden history in the cluster.
hidden_events.record_implications: Ho-Chunk families appear in federal census records inconsistently — sometimes enumerated on Indian schedules, sometimes missed entirely, sometimes listed on general population schedules without tribal identification. County records (deeds, probate, vital records) largely exclude Ho-Chunk people because they were not recognized as county residents in the legal sense until well into the twentieth century. BIA records and tribal enrollment records are the primary documentary source. Wisconsin Ho-Chunk tribal records are maintained by the Ho-Chunk Nation at Black River Falls.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: The Ho-Chunk walked back to the Driftless after every removal — from Iowa, from Minnesota, from South Dakota — because the coulees were home in a way that no government order could cancel. {ancestor_name}'s county records do not mention them, but they were there, living in the margins of the landscape that had been theirs.
hidden_events.dignity_note: The Ho-Chunk story is one of extraordinary resilience, not victimhood. Multiple forced removals failed because Ho-Chunk families chose to return, repeatedly, at great personal cost. Frame this as resistance and persistence, not as passive suffering.
hidden_events.source: Loew, Patty. Indian Nations of Wisconsin: Histories of Endurance and Renewal. 2nd ed. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2013. Lurie, Nancy Oestreich. Wisconsin Indians. Rev. ed. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2002.

hidden_events.event_name: Black Hawk War and the Bad Axe Massacre
hidden_events.description: The Black Hawk War of 1832 was the event that opened the Southern Driftless to white settlement. Black Hawk's band of Sauk and Meskwaki crossed the Mississippi into Illinois in spring 1832, seeking to reclaim ceded lands. Pursued by militia and federal troops through southern Wisconsin, the band was cornered at the confluence of the Bad Axe River and the Mississippi in Crawford County (just north of the cluster). The Battle of Bad Axe (August 1-2, 1832) was a massacre — soldiers and the armed steamboat Warrior killed men, women, and children attempting to cross the river. Estimates range from 150 to 300 killed. The war's aftermath opened the lead district and the entire Southern Driftless to unrestricted white settlement and triggered the Ho-Chunk removals.
hidden_events.record_implications: The Black Hawk War is well-documented from the military side — muster rolls, pension records, and published accounts survive. Indigenous casualties are poorly documented. Militia service records for Wisconsin volunteers are at the National Archives and the Wisconsin Historical Society. Some early Driftless settlers were Black Hawk War veterans who took land in the region they had marched through.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: The land that {ancestor_name} settled in {county} County was open for settlement because of what happened at Bad Axe in 1832 — the war that cleared the Driftless for white farms was fought through the same coulees where the family would plow.
hidden_events.dignity_note: Bad Axe was a massacre of people — including women and children — attempting to flee across a river. Do not sanitize it as a 'battle' without acknowledging the nature of the violence. Use 'massacre' when describing the killing of noncombatants.
hidden_events.source: Jung, Patrick J. The Black Hawk War of 1832. Norman: University of Oklahoma Press, 2007. Trask, Kerry A. Black Hawk: The Battle for the Heart of America. New York: Henry Holt, 2006.

hidden_events.event_name: Lead Poisoning in the Mining District
hidden_events.description: The lead mining district that gave Wisconsin its 'Badger State' nickname extracted millions of pounds of galena (lead sulfide) from shallow diggings and deeper shaft mines across Iowa, Lafayette, and Grant counties. The health costs were severe and largely unacknowledged in the historical record. Lead dust contaminated miners, their families, and the water supply. Smelting operations released lead fumes across the mining towns. No health regulations existed. Miners who survived the physical dangers of underground work faced chronic lead poisoning — 'painter's colic,' tremors, cognitive decline, kidney failure. The Cornish miners who brought their expertise from Cornwall's tin and copper mines traded one set of occupational hazards for another. Mining-town mortality rates were higher than surrounding agricultural communities, but cause-of-death records from this era rarely identified lead exposure.
hidden_events.record_implications: Death records from the mining era (pre-1860) rarely specify lead poisoning as cause of death. Look for elevated mortality rates in mining townships compared to agricultural townships in the same counties. Cemetery records in Mineral Point, Shullsburg, and Linden may show shorter lifespans for mining families. Church burial records sometimes note 'miner' as occupation, allowing mortality comparisons.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: The lead that made {ancestor_name}'s living in the mining district was also what shortened it — lead dust in the lungs, lead in the water, lead fumes from the smelter that hung over Mineral Point on still days. The death records do not say 'lead poisoning,' but the miners' graves say it plainly enough.
hidden_events.dignity_note: Mining families accepted these risks because the alternatives were worse — unemployment in Cornwall, subsistence farming on marginal land. Do not frame miners as ignorant of danger; frame them as people making rational choices within constrained options.
hidden_events.source: Schafer, Joseph. The Wisconsin Lead Region. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1932. Nesbit, Robert C. Wisconsin: A History. 2nd ed. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1989.

hidden_events.event_name: Anti-German Hysteria During World War I
hidden_events.description: Wisconsin had the largest German-born population of any state, and the Southern Driftless — particularly Sauk County (Sauk City, Prairie du Sac) and Dane County (Madison) — had deeply rooted German communities with German-language newspapers, churches, schools, and cultural organizations. When the United States entered World War I in 1917, anti-German hysteria swept the state. German-language instruction was banned in public schools. German-language newspapers were pressured to cease publication or switch to English. German cultural organizations (Turnverein, singing societies) disbanded or anglicized. Families anglicized surnames — Schmidt became Smith, Mueller became Miller. Senator Robert La Follette, who had opposed the war, was burned in effigy and nearly expelled from the Senate. The Wisconsin State Council of Defense organized loyalty campaigns that targeted German-speaking communities. In Sauk City, where German had been the language of daily life for seventy years, the war forced a linguistic and cultural rupture that was never fully repaired.
hidden_events.record_implications: The anti-German period created a documentary gap — German-language records (newspapers, church records, organizational minutes) largely cease between 1917 and 1920. Surname changes during this period can break genealogical chains. Church records that had been kept in German switched to English, sometimes mid-volume. Look for Wisconsin State Council of Defense records at the Wisconsin Historical Society for documentation of loyalty investigations.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: In 1916, {ancestor_name} spoke German at church, read a German newspaper, and sang in the Maennerchor. By 1918, the German newspaper had closed, the church had switched to English, and the Maennerchor had disbanded — the war made the language of home into the language of the enemy.
hidden_events.dignity_note: German Americans were coerced into abandoning their language and culture by state-sponsored pressure campaigns. Do not frame this as voluntary assimilation — it was forced cultural suppression during wartime hysteria.
hidden_events.source: Kazal, Russell A. Becoming Old Stock: The Paradox of German-American Identity. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2004. Nesbit, Wisconsin: A History (1989). Luebke, Frederick C. Bonds of Loyalty: German-Americans and World War I. DeKalb: Northern Illinois University Press, 1974.

hidden_events.description: In February and May 1933, dairy farmers across southern Wisconsin — including the Southern Driftless counties — went on strike, blockading roads to prevent milk from reaching processing plants and urban markets. The strikes were driven by catastrophically low milk prices during the Depression — farmers were receiving less per hundredweight than it cost to produce. The Wisconsin Cooperative Milk Pool organized the strikes, which turned violent when non-striking farmers attempted to run the blockades. Milk was dumped on roads. Trucks were overturned. Governor Albert Schmedeman called out the National Guard. In the Driftless counties, the strikes were particularly intense because dairy farming was the sole cash economy — there was nothing else. The strikes achieved modest price increases but exposed the vulnerability of an economy built entirely on a single commodity.
hidden_events.record_implications: Newspaper accounts are the primary source — the Wisconsin State Journal and local county newspapers covered the strikes extensively. Wisconsin Cooperative Milk Pool records are at the Wisconsin Historical Society. National Guard deployment records document the military response. Court records in affected counties document arrests of strike participants.
hidden_events.narrative_hook: In 1933, {ancestor_name} watched neighbors dump milk on the road rather than sell it for less than it cost to produce — the Driftless dairy economy had come to the point where destroying the product was more rational than delivering it.
hidden_events.dignity_note: The milk strikers were desperate farmers, not radicals. Frame the strikes as an economic survival response, not as political extremism.
hidden_events.source: Apps, Jerry. Wisconsin Agriculture: A History. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2015. Nesbit, Wisconsin: A History (1989). Dyson, Lowell K. 'The Milk Strikes of 1933.' Wisconsin Magazine of History 66, no. 4 (Summer 1983): 280-299.

systemic_erasures.description: Ho-Chunk people lived in the Southern Driftless throughout the period covered by county records, but they appear in those records sporadically at best. They were not landowners in the county deed system (until allotments in the twentieth century), were not consistently enumerated in county-administered censuses, and were not recorded in county vital records systems. Their presence in the landscape was continuous; their presence in the county archive is fragmentary. This is not an accident — it reflects a legal system that did not recognize indigenous people as participants in the county governance structure.
systemic_erasures.what_is_missing: Land ownership records, vital records (births, marriages, deaths), probate records, tax records, school enrollment records — the standard genealogical paper trail that exists for white settlers is largely absent for Ho-Chunk families in county repositories.
systemic_erasures.where_to_look_instead: Bureau of Indian Affairs (BIA) records at the National Archives — Great Lakes Agency records. Ho-Chunk Nation tribal enrollment records at Black River Falls, WI. Federal Indian census schedules (separate from general population schedules). Wisconsin Historical Society Ho-Chunk collections.
systemic_erasures.source: Loew, Indian Nations of Wisconsin (2013). Lurie, Wisconsin Indians (2002).

systemic_erasures.description: Wisconsin did not require statewide vital records registration until 1907 (with effective compliance lagging into the 1910s). Before 1907, vital records were supposed to be kept by county registers of deeds, but compliance was inconsistent. In the Driftless interior counties (Richland, Grant, Iowa), compliance was particularly poor — isolated farming communities had little contact with county government, and many births, marriages, and deaths went unrecorded. Church records, cemetery records, and newspaper notices are the primary substitutes for this gap period.
systemic_erasures.what_is_missing: Birth records, death records, and (to a lesser extent) marriage records before 1907. The gap is worst in the most isolated counties (Richland, interior Grant, interior Iowa) and best in the most accessible counties (Dane, Rock).
systemic_erasures.where_to_look_instead: Church records (Norwegian Lutheran, Swiss Reformed, Cornish Methodist, German Lutheran and Catholic congregations kept their own vital records). Cemetery headstones. County newspaper notices (especially for marriages and deaths). Federal census records (especially 1850-1900, which record family composition). WPA transcriptions of early records at the Wisconsin Historical Society.
systemic_erasures.source: Danky, James P., and Sandra Koeppe. Guide to Wisconsin Newspapers, 1833-1957. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1982. Wisconsin Historical Society vital records guides.

systemic_erasures.erasure_name: Postwar Small Farm Consolidation and Disappearance
systemic_erasures.description: The postwar period saw a dramatic acceleration of farm consolidation in the Driftless counties. Small dairy farms — 40 to 80 acres on ridgetops and in coulees — could not compete with larger operations on flatter land. The GI Bill pulled a generation of farm sons to UW Madison and non-agricultural careers. The farms they left behind were absorbed into larger operations or abandoned. By 1960, the number of farms in the Driftless counties had dropped by a third from the 1940 peak. This consolidation erased not just farms but entire rural neighborhoods — one-room schools closed, crossroads stores closed, rural churches consolidated. The documentary trail of these disappearing communities — school records, store ledgers, church minutes — was often lost when the institutions closed.
systemic_erasures.what_is_missing: One-room school records (attendance rolls, teacher reports). Crossroads store ledgers. Small rural church records (when congregations dissolved, records were sometimes transferred to the surviving merged congregation, sometimes lost). Creamery and cheese factory records from small operations that closed.
systemic_erasures.where_to_look_instead: County superintendent of schools records (some preserved at county historical societies). Wisconsin Historical Society manuscript collections (some rural institutional records were donated). Local historical society collections in county seats. USDA agricultural census data (documents the statistical decline).
systemic_erasures.source: Apps, Jerry. Wisconsin Agriculture: A History (2015). Danbom, David B. Born in the Country: A History of Rural America. 2nd ed. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 2006.

underground_networks.description: Southern Wisconsin was an active corridor of the Underground Railroad, with routes running north from Illinois through Rock, Dane, and Green counties toward Lake Michigan ports and Canada. Milton (Rock County) was a major station — the Milton House, an inn operated by Joseph Goodrich, had a tunnel connecting to a log cabin used to shelter freedom seekers. Janesville, Madison, and Waukesha were documented stations. The network was supported by Yankee settlers with abolitionist convictions — the same Congregational and Free-Soil communities that had migrated from the Burned-Over District of western New York. Wisconsin's resistance to the Fugitive Slave Act of 1850 — particularly the Joshua Glover case in 1854, when a Milwaukee crowd freed an escaped enslaved man from federal custody — reflected the strength of antislavery sentiment in the state.
underground_networks.record_implications: Underground Railroad activity was deliberately undocumented — participants kept no records to protect freedom seekers and themselves. Evidence comes from postwar memoirs, oral traditions recorded decades later, and architectural evidence (the Milton House tunnel). Newspaper accounts from the era document the public antislavery movement but not the clandestine network. The Joshua Glover case generated extensive court records.
underground_networks.narrative_hook: The Milton House in Rock County had a tunnel under the floor — {ancestor_name}'s neighbors may have known about it, or may have used it, but nobody wrote it down because writing it down could send people back to slavery.
underground_networks.source: Switala, William J. Underground Railroad in Wisconsin. Mechanicsburg, PA: Stackpole Books, 2012. Nesbit, Wisconsin: A History (1989).

underground_networks.description: Robert M. La Follette Sr.'s Progressive movement, centered in Madison and Dane County, built a political network that reached into every Driftless county through a combination of electoral organization, newspaper support, university extension education, and farmer cooperative institutions. The 'Wisconsin Idea' — the principle that the university should serve the entire state — was the intellectual framework, and the cooperative creameries, mutual insurance companies, and farmers' organizations were the practical infrastructure. The Progressive network was not secret, but its depth and organizational sophistication were not fully visible in conventional political records. The network connected Scandinavian farmers in the western Driftless to German freethinkers in Sauk County to Yankee reformers in Dane County — an unlikely coalition united by opposition to railroad monopolies and political machines.
underground_networks.record_implications: Progressive political records are extensive — La Follette papers at the Wisconsin Historical Society, newspaper archives (especially La Follette's Weekly Magazine), UW Extension records, cooperative organization records. The network is well-documented but understanding which families participated requires cross-referencing political affiliation (primary election records, party registration where available) with cooperative membership lists and newspaper subscription rolls.
underground_networks.narrative_hook: La Follette's Progressives organized the Driftless one creamery meeting at a time — {ancestor_name}'s cooperative membership was political as much as economic, a vote for the Wisconsin Idea delivered through a milk check.
underground_networks.source: Unger, Nancy C. Fighting Bob La Follette: The Righteous Reformer. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2000. Nesbit, Wisconsin: A History (1989). Carstensen, Vernon. Farms or Forests: Evolution of a State Land Policy for Northern Wisconsin, 1850-1932. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1958.

reframing_narratives.standard_narrative: The Southern Driftless was 'opened for settlement' after the Black Hawk War, with pioneers arriving to find unoccupied wilderness ready for farming.
reframing_narratives.reframed_narrative: The Southern Driftless was Ho-Chunk homeland, occupied and managed for centuries before white settlement. The Black Hawk War and subsequent removal treaties did not open 'empty' land — they forcibly displaced an existing population. Ho-Chunk families continued to live in the Driftless throughout the settlement era, and their continued presence contradicts the 'empty land' narrative that county histories typically present.
reframing_narratives.evidence: Ho-Chunk place names throughout the Driftless (Wisconsin = 'Wees-koos-rah,' red stone). Archaeological sites across all eight counties. Federal removal records documenting forced displacement. Ho-Chunk return migration documented in BIA records.
reframing_narratives.source: Loew, Indian Nations of Wisconsin (2013). Birmingham, Robert A., and Leslie E. Eisenberg. Indian Mounds of Wisconsin. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 2000.

reframing_narratives.standard_narrative: The lead mining district brought prosperity to southwestern Wisconsin, attracting skilled Cornish miners who built thriving communities.
reframing_narratives.reframed_narrative: The lead district did bring economic activity, but at severe health costs that the prosperity narrative omits. Lead poisoning affected miners, their families, and communities near smelting operations. The Cornish miners' expertise came from Cornish mines that were themselves health hazards. The mining towns' prosperity was built on extracted mineral wealth that left contaminated land and shortened lives. The 'Badger State' nickname celebrates an industry that poisoned its workers.
reframing_narratives.evidence: Elevated mortality rates in mining townships compared to agricultural townships in the same counties. Mining-town cemetery records showing shorter lifespans. The decline of mining towns after the surface lead played out, leaving communities without the resource that had sustained them.
reframing_narratives.source: Schafer, The Wisconsin Lead Region (1932). Nesbit, Wisconsin: A History (1989).

reframing_narratives.standard_narrative: German Americans gradually assimilated into mainstream American culture, adopting English and American customs over several generations.
reframing_narratives.reframed_narrative: German cultural institutions in the Driftless — newspapers, schools, churches, singing societies, Turnverein — were not abandoned voluntarily over generations. They were suppressed during World War I by state-sponsored loyalty campaigns, legal prohibition of German-language instruction, and social pressure that made German cultural expression dangerous. The shift from German to English in Sauk City happened in two years (1917-1919), not over two generations. What looks like assimilation in the historical record was coercion.
reframing_narratives.evidence: Wisconsin State Council of Defense records documenting anti-German campaigns. German-language newspaper cessation dates clustering in 1917-1918. Church record language switches from German to English mid-volume in the WWI period. Surname anglicization records.
reframing_narratives.source: Luebke, Bonds of Loyalty (1974). Kazal, Becoming Old Stock (2004).

reframing_narratives.standard_narrative: Small Driftless farms were replaced by larger operations through natural market forces — more efficient farms outcompeted less efficient ones.
reframing_narratives.reframed_narrative: The disappearance of small Driftless dairy farms after 1945 was driven by federal agricultural policy (price supports favoring large operations), university extension advice promoting expansion and mechanization, and lending practices that favored consolidation. The Driftless terrain — small ridgetop fields, steep coulees, winding roads — made large-scale operation difficult, but policy was designed for flat-land agriculture. Families did not choose to leave farming; the economic structure was redesigned to make small farming impossible.
reframing_narratives.evidence: USDA agricultural census data showing farm number decline and average farm size increase. Federal milk marketing order records. UW Extension bulletins promoting farm consolidation. Farm Credit Administration lending records favoring larger operations.
reframing_narratives.source: Apps, Wisconsin Agriculture (2015). Danbom, Born in the Country (2006).

record_gap_explanations.gap_description: Wisconsin vital records before 1907 are incomplete
record_gap_explanations.explanation: Wisconsin did not mandate statewide vital records registration until 1907, and effective compliance lagged into the 1910s. Before 1907, county registers of deeds were supposed to record births, marriages, and deaths, but compliance varied widely. In the isolated Driftless interior counties (Richland, Grant, Iowa), compliance was particularly poor. Many births happened at home with no physician present and were never recorded. Deaths on remote ridgetop farms might be noted only in a church burial register or a cemetery headstone.
record_gap_explanations.workaround: Use church records as the primary substitute — Norwegian Lutheran, Swiss Reformed, Cornish Methodist, German Lutheran and Catholic congregations maintained their own vital records. Cemetery records, newspaper notices, and federal census records (especially the 1900 and 1910 censuses, which record year of birth and number of children born/surviving) fill additional gaps.

record_gap_explanations.gap_description: Ho-Chunk families largely absent from county records
record_gap_explanations.explanation: Ho-Chunk people lived in the Driftless throughout the settlement era but were not participants in the county legal system — they did not own land through the county deed system, were not consistently counted in county-administered records, and were not recorded in county vital records. This is a structural absence, not an accidental one. The county record system was designed for white settlers and did not accommodate indigenous people who existed outside its legal framework.
record_gap_explanations.workaround: Bureau of Indian Affairs records at the National Archives (Great Lakes Agency). Ho-Chunk Nation tribal enrollment records. Federal Indian census schedules (separate from general population schedules). Wisconsin Historical Society Ho-Chunk manuscript collections. Oral history collections at the Ho-Chunk Nation archives.

record_gap_explanations.gap_description: Territorial-era records (1836-1848) are scattered across multiple repositories
record_gap_explanations.explanation: Before Wisconsin statehood in 1848, the Southern Driftless was part of Michigan Territory (to 1836), then Wisconsin Territory. Territorial records — land transactions, court proceedings, mining claims, military records — are split between the National Archives, the Wisconsin Historical Society, county courthouses, and the Michigan state archives. The lead mining district had the earliest settlement (1827), but the records from the pre-territorial and territorial periods are fragmentary. Mining claims were often informal — a miner staked a claim and worked it without filing paperwork. The earliest land records may be at the federal land office (now at the National Archives) rather than the county courthouse.
record_gap_explanations.workaround: Federal land entry files at the National Archives (GLO records, now available through BLM General Land Office Records). Wisconsin Historical Society territorial records collections. Iowa County courthouse (the oldest county, organized 1829) for the earliest county-level records. Schafer's The Wisconsin Lead Region (1932) provides extensive documentation of early mining-era records and their locations.

dignity_notes.guidance: The Ho-Chunk story is one of resilience and resistance. Multiple federal removal attempts failed because Ho-Chunk families chose to return to the Driftless, walking hundreds of miles repeatedly. Frame this as agency and determination, not as victimhood. The Ho-Chunk did not passively endure removal — they actively refused it.

The killing at Bad Axe included women, children, and elderly people attempting to cross the Mississippi River. Use 'massacre' rather than 'battle' when describing the killing of noncombatants. Acknowledge that this violence was the precondition for white settlement in the Driftless.

Mining families accepted occupational hazards because the alternatives — unemployment, subsistence farming, starvation — were worse. Do not portray miners as ignorant or reckless. Frame lead exposure as a structural consequence of an unregulated industry, not as individual failure.

German Americans did not voluntarily abandon their language and culture. They were coerced by state-sponsored loyalty campaigns, legal prohibitions on German-language instruction, and social pressure that equated German cultural expression with disloyalty. Use 'coerced' or 'forced,' not 'chose' or 'decided.'

The striking dairy farmers were not radicals — they were family farmers facing prices below the cost of production. Frame the strikes as economic desperation, not political ideology. The violence that occurred was a consequence of impossible economic conditions, not of radical intent.

Families who left farming after 1945 did not fail — they were pushed out by policy choices that favored large-scale agriculture on flat terrain. The Driftless landscape that had sustained small dairy farms for generations was not designed for the industrial agriculture that federal policy promoted. Frame departure as structural displacement, not as personal inadequacy.

narrative_hooks: The county records tell you who owned the Driftless land after 1832, but they do not tell you who lived on it before — the Ho-Chunk presence is written in the place names, not in the deed books.

What the Driftless archive does not say is as important as what it does — the lead miners' health, the Ho-Chunk persistence, the German cultural surrender, the small farm forced out by policy rather than failure.

The anti-German campaign of 1917-1918 left a gap in the Driftless archive — German-language newspapers stopped, German church records switched to English mid-volume, and a generation's cultural expression was erased in two years.

{ancestor_name}'s family history in the Driftless may have a gap where the records should be — not because the family was not there, but because the record system was not designed to see everyone who was.
source_references: Loew, Patty. Indian Nations of Wisconsin: Histories of Endurance and Renewal. 2nd ed. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2013.
Lurie, Nancy Oestreich. Wisconsin Indians. Rev. ed. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2002.
Jung, Patrick J. The Black Hawk War of 1832. Norman: University of Oklahoma Press, 2007.
Trask, Kerry A. Black Hawk: The Battle for the Heart of America. New York: Henry Holt, 2006.
Schafer, Joseph. The Wisconsin Lead Region. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1932.
Nesbit, Robert C. Wisconsin: A History. 2nd ed. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1989.
Luebke, Frederick C. Bonds of Loyalty: German-Americans and World War I. DeKalb: Northern Illinois University Press, 1974.
Kazal, Russell A. Becoming Old Stock: The Paradox of German-American Identity. Princeton: Princeton University Press, 2004.
Apps, Jerry. Wisconsin Agriculture: A History. Madison: Wisconsin Historical Society Press, 2015.
Danbom, David B. Born in the Country: A History of Rural America. 2nd ed. Baltimore: Johns Hopkins University Press, 2006.
Switala, William J. Underground Railroad in Wisconsin. Mechanicsburg, PA: Stackpole Books, 2012.
Unger, Nancy C. Fighting Bob La Follette: The Righteous Reformer. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2000.
Birmingham, Robert A., and Leslie E. Eisenberg. Indian Mounds of Wisconsin. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 2000.
Danky, James P., and Sandra Koeppe. Guide to Wisconsin Newspapers, 1833-1957. Madison: State Historical Society of Wisconsin, 1982.
